coredata.dc:title: A chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy for rapid fluorometric detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate
coredata.dc:creator: He, Jincan
Su, Yuyuan
Sun, Zijun
Zhang, Runkun
Wu, Fuhai
Bai, Yan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a fluorometric method based on chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy was proposed for rapid detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS). The method can be performed by two steps of simple physical mix. The whole procedure takes no more than 25 min. In pH 5.50 Britton-Robinson buffer solution, a green fluorescent dye (trisodium-8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonate, HPTS) reacted with protonated chitosan to form complex by electrostatic attraction, leading in the strong quenching of HPTS. In the presence of PFOS, the fluorescence of HPTS recovered resulting from the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between PFOS and chitosan. The spectral characteristics, the reaction mechanisms of fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery, and the analytical conditions were investigated. Under the optimum conditions, a fluorometric method based on the chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy was developed for detection of PFOS. The value of fluorescence restoration (ΔF) was proportional to the concentration of PFOS in range of 0.0050–2.0 μmol/L with correlation coefficient of 0.9949. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.0 nmol/L. The method was applied to the determination of PFOS in environment water with recoveries of 90.8–102% and RSD of 2.8–5.9%. The fluorometric method based on chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy is simple, rapid, sensitive and economical, showing potentiality for detection of PFOS in environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perfluorooctane sulfonate
Fluorescence
Trisodium-8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonate
Chitosan
Turn-on

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application to PFOS determination in environment water
Comparing the method with other methods

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province
Guangzhou Municipal Science and Technology Project
National Natural Science Foundation of China
Medical Science and Technology Foundation of Guangdong Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the fluorometric method based on CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy for detection of PFOS.
The fluorescence spectra (a) and the absorption spectra (b) of different solution.
(a) Zeta potential of CTS, HPTS-CTS and HPTS-CTS-PFOS, (b) Fluorescence decay curves of HPTS, HPTS-CTS and HPTS-CTS-PFOS.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra with different concentration of PFOS (0, 0.0050, 0.010, 0.50, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 μmol/L), (b) Calibration curve between PFOS concentration and the enhanced fluorescence intensity.
Tolerance ratio of various substances to the determination of 1.0 μmol/L PFOS.
Determination of PFOS in environmental water with the fluorometric method based on CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sample 1: the river water gained from the Zhujiang River in Zhongda dock.

row.entry: Tap water, river water and mountain water

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration was calculated with relative molecular weight of PFOS (C8HF17O3S) as 500.

head.ce:title: A chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy for rapid fluorometric detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Public Health Detection and Assessment, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou 510310, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Public Health Detection and Assessment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Public Health Detection and Assessment, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou 510310, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou 510310, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou 510310, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: No. 283 Jianghai Avenue, Haizhu District, Guangzhou 510310, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a fluorometric method based on chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy was proposed for rapid detection of perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS). The method can be performed by two steps of simple physical mix. The whole procedure takes no more than 25 min. In pH 5.50 Britton-Robinson buffer solution, a green fluorescent dye (trisodium-8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonate, HPTS) reacted with protonated chitosan to form complex by electrostatic attraction, leading in the strong quenching of HPTS. In the presence of PFOS, the fluorescence of HPTS recovered resulting from the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between PFOS and chitosan. The spectral characteristics, the reaction mechanisms of fluorescence quenching and fluorescence recovery, and the analytical conditions were investigated. Under the optimum conditions, a fluorometric method based on the chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy was developed for detection of PFOS. The value of fluorescence restoration (ΔF) was proportional to the concentration of PFOS in range of 0.0050–2.0 μmol/L with correlation coefficient of 0.9949. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.0 nmol/L. The method was applied to the determination of PFOS in environment water with recoveries of 90.8–102% and RSD of 2.8–5.9%. The fluorometric method based on chitosan-mediated “turn-on” strategy is simple, rapid, sensitive and economical, showing potentiality for detection of PFOS in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) is a representative synthetic fluorinated organic compound featured with a fluorinated alkyl chain and a terminal sulfonic group [1]. It is extensively applied in manufacturing processes such as textiles, leathers, cosmetics, firefighting foams and packing materials since its first use in 1950s [2]. Due to high energy carbon–fluorine (C–F) bond, PFOS shows great stability and resistance to degrade in the environment [3,4]. Once PFOS enters the environmental media (e.g., atmosphere, water, sludge, soil), it will be hard to break down [5]. Moreover, PFOS can be transferred, accumulated and magnified along with the food chain [6]. It has been reported that PFOS was found in a variety of human beings and animals around the world, even in the Arctic and Antarctic [7,8]. PFOS accumulating in the organisms brings noticeable harm to human health [9,10]. Toxicology research has demonstrated that PFOS can cause hepatotoxicity, immunotoxicity, development toxicity or neurotoxicity to human and animals [11,12]. In consideration of environmental pollution hazards and human health risks, PFOS has been listed as one of the persistent organic pollutants (POPs) by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) in 2009 [13]. So far, various organizations in Europe [14] and America [15] have regulated the levels of PFOS in drinking water, respectively. Therefore, the monitoring of PFOS is urgently required.

Currently, the major analytical methods for the detection of PFOS are chromatography technologies, including liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [16–18], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS-MS) [19–21], and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [22,23], etc. Despite of good sensitivity and accuracy, the chromatography-based methods require expensive equipment, complicated operation and long time. Therefore, the chromatography-based methods are not suitable for detection of PFOS quickly or on-site. The simple, rapid and economical method is highly desirable. Recently, rapid analytical techniques for the determination of PFOS are emerging, such as colorimetric [24–26], fluorescent [27,28], electrochemical [29,30] and photoelectrochemical [31] quantification.

Fluorescence spectrometry has attracted considerable attention due to advantages of simplicity, high sensitivity and low-cost [32]. It has been widely applied to analysis of analytes including metal ions, organic molecules, proteins and DNA. Trisodium-8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonate (HPTS) is a commercially available and water-soluble fluorescent dye with high quantum yield. HPTS shows inherit pH-responsive property due to one phenolic hydroxyl group (protonated state or deprotonated state) in the molecule. With the unique property, HPTS has been used as a fluorescent probe for various sensing applications such as pH, carbon dioxide, caffeine, arginine, lysine and chitooligosaccharides [33–38].

In our previous study, we have demonstrated that the chitooligosaccharides can react with HPTS to form an ion-association complex, causing strong fluorescence quenching of the HPTS [38]. Chitosan (CTS), as the precursor of chitooligosaccharides, is a natural cation polymer characterized with hydrophobicity, high adsorbability and biocompatibility [39,40]. So, it is speculated that CTS should also have the capability of fluorescence-quenching to HPTS and be apt to combine with PFOS due to the high adsorbability and hydrophobicity of CTS. Inspired by these points, a fluorometric method based on CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy was proposed for detection of PFOS. In this strategy, CTS acts as a quencher for HPTS to quench the fluorescence of HPTS. When PFOS is added, the fluorescence of HPTS recovers due to the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between PFOS and CTS. The spectral characteristics, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching and the fluorescence recovery, and the analytical conditions were investigated. Under the selected conditions, a fluorometric method based on the CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy for detection of PFOS was developed. The applicability of the method to the determination of PFOS in real samples was evaluated.

Acetic acid (98%) were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Boric acid (99.5%), chitosan (CTS, deacetylation degree of 95%, and viscosity of 100–200 mPa s), sodium dodecylsulfate (SDS, 99%), sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS, 98%) and hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB, 99%) were bought from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (98%) was obtained from Guangzhou chemical reagent factory (Guangzhou, China). Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS, 99%) and trisodium-8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonate (HPTS, 96%) were bought from Shanghai Anpel Laboratory Technologies In C. (Shanghai, China). All the other chemicals were analytical grade in this study and were used without further purification. The ultrapure water (18 MΩ/cm) was used throughout the study.

A PHS-3C PH indicator (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was used for adjusting pH. A CP124C electronic analytical balance (Ohaus, USA) was used for weighing. Fluorescence measurements were performed on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible spectra were obtained from a UV-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, China). The steady-state fluorescence spectra were collected on a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The zeta potential value was measured on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK).

A 20 mL of CTS solution, 10 mL of HPTS and 70 mL of ultrapure water were respectively added into a brown flacon and kept for a while, so that the mixed quenching solution was obtained. Afterwards, a 2 mL of the mixed quenching solution, appropriate PFOS solution and a 1.0 mL of Britton-Robinson (BR) solution were sequentially added into a 10.0 mL calibrated flask. The mixture was diluted to the scale line with ultrapure water, followed by mixing well. Several minutes later, the fluorescence spectra of the final complex were scanned (λex = 405 nm) and the fluorescence intensity at 512 nm was recorded. The change intensity (ΔF = F − F 0) was calculated, where F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of the final complex and the reagent blank, respectively.

The principle of the CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy for detection of PFOS is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The fluorescence of HPTS is firstly quenched by CTS. In the presence of PFOS, CTS changes to bind with PFOS due to strong interactions between CTS and PFOS, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of HPTS.

When HPTS was excited with the wavelength of 405 nm, it exhibited a strong fluorescence with the maximum emission peak at 512 nm (Fig. S1). The fluorescence emission spectra of HPTS, CTS, PFOS, HPTS-CTS and HPTS-CTS-PFOS were collected with the exciting wavelength of 405 nm. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, HPTS has strong fluorescence emission at 512 nm. After adding CTS into HPTS, the fluorescence intensity of HPTS decreases significantly. Upon addition of PFOS, the fluorescence intensity recovers greatly. Moreover, as the concentration of PFOS increases, the level of fluorescence recovery also increases.

Firstly, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was investigated. On one hand, Fig. 2b depicts the effect of CTS and PFOS on the absorption spectra of HPTS in aqueous solution. The HPTS has three strong characteristic absorption peaks at 288 nm, 369 nm and 402 nm. The intensity of adsorption peaks becomes weak in the presence of CTS. It illustrated that HPTS may react with CTS to form HPTS-CTS complex, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of HPTS. Obviously, in pH 5.5 BR buffer solution, HPTS was fully ionized and existed as trivalent anion, while CTS with abundant amino groups was protonated with much positive charges. Therefore, it was speculated that the electrostatic interaction played an important role on fluorescence quenching. The zeta potential measurement was performed to confirm it. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the zeta potential of CTS with +8.43 mV changes to HPTS-CTS system with +6.85 mV, demonstrating the electrostatic interaction between HPTS and CTS. On the other hand, CTS with much amino groups can act as electron donor, while HPTS with benzene rings can play as an electron acceptor. So, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between HPTS and CTS may also contribute to the fluorescence quenching. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of HPTS shortens after the addition of CTS (Fig. 3b), indicating the little possibility for the static quenching. Hardly any overlap is observed between the absorption spectrum of CTS and the fluorescence spectrum of HPTS (Fig. S2), demonstrating the less chance for the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between HPTS and CTS.

Secondly, the mechanism of fluorescence recovery was also investigated. As displayed in Fig. 2a, the level of fluorescence recovery depends on the concentration of PFOS. The more PFOS was added, the more fluorescence recovery was obtained. It showed that CTS held higher affinity toward PFOS than HPTS. With hydrophobic and lipophobic properties, PFOS can react with the positively charged CTS by electrostatic and hydrophobic interaction. Therefore, the active sites in CTS were replaced by PFOS, and free HPTS anions were released from HPTS-CTS complex. The deduction was supported by UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2b), zeta potential value (Fig. 3a) and fluorescence delay characterization (Fig. 3b). After adding PFOS to the system of HPTS-CTS, the absorbance of HPTS increases with the increasing of PFOS concentration (Fig. 2b). The zeta potential value turns from +6.85 mV (HPTS-CTS) to −21.53 mV (HPTS-CTS-PFOS) (Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of HPTS-CTS-PFOS system becomes longer than HPTS-CTS system (Fig. 3b).

To achieve the favorable performance for analysis of PFOS, various conditions (such as concentration of CTS, buffer solution, pH, quenching time, recovery time, temperature and ion strength) that may affect the assay system were investigated.

In this system, CTS was used as a fluorescence quencher and its concentration played an important role on quenching efficiency (QE). The concentration of CTS was investigated firstly. As depicted in Fig. S3, the QE depends on the concentration of CTS. The QE increases with the concentration of CTS until the concentration achieves 1.5 mg/L. With further increasing of CTS concentration, the QE shows a slow downward trend. The QE is more than 90% with CTS concentration of 1.5–2.5 mg/L. When PFOS was respectively added into three quenching system of CTS-HTPS with different concentration of CTS (1.5, 2.0, 2.5 mg/L), the highest efficiency of fluorescence recovery was found in CTS-HTPS with 2.0 mg/L of CTS. Therefore, 2.0 mg/L was selected as the concentration of CTS for the following study.

The effects of buffer solutions (BR and HAc-NaAc) on the fluorescence recovery of HPTS were investigated. As depicted in Fig. S4, in BR buffer solution at pH 7.0, the fluorescence recovery was negative (column e). Either in BR buffer solution or HAc-NaAc buffer solution at pH 4.0–5.5 (column a–c and column f–h), the value of fluorescence recovery (F − F 0) increases rapidly with the increasing of pH value. When the pH value is more than 5.5, the value of fluorescence recovery decreases with the further increasing of pH value (column d and e). It was mainly due to that the fluorescence intensity of HPTS-CTS system increase with the increasing of pH, and the extent enhanced when the pH value was more than 5.5. Therefore, the BR buffer solution with pH 5.5 was the best choice.

The quenching time for incubation of CTS and HPTS was studied. As shown in Fig. S5, after mixing of HPTS and CTS, the fluorescence intensity of HPTS decreases quickly within 10 min. After 10 min, the fluorescence intensity maintains within 60 min. Therefore, the HPTS-CTS system can be used after quenching for 10–60 min.

The effects of recovery time for PFOS and HPTS-CTS system on fluorescence were also investigated. By collecting the fluorescence intensity at 512 nm at intervals of different reaction time, we found that the fluorescence of the HPTS-CTS system enhanced and kept stable at least for 60 min after the addition of PFOS (Fig. S6). To guarantee the completion of reaction, the fluorescence measures were performed after reaction for 10 min.

The effects of temperature ranging from 25 to 60 °C on the fluorescence intensity was studied. As displayed in Fig. S7, when the temperature is between 25 °C and 45 °C, the fluorescence recovery value changes little. However, when the temperature is higher than 45 °C, the fluorescence intensity increases sharply. As the increasing of temperature, the thermal motion of molecules and the collision frequency between molecules intensify, resulting in the dissociation of HPTS-CTS complex and following with fluorescence enhancement of the system. At high temperature, the background signal rose, and the stability of the system became poor. Considering the sensitivity and stability, all experiments were conducted at room temperature (25 °C).

To evaluate the effect of ion strength on the fluorescence intensity of the system, a series of 0.2 mol/L NaCl solution were added into the system. As exhibited in Fig. S8, with the increasing of NaCl concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the HPTS-CTS system increases gradually, indicating the electrostatic attraction between CTS and HPTS. As the increasing of NaCl concentration, the effect of the electrostatic shielding of charges enhanced, thus weakening the combination between CTS and HPTS and causing the increasing of fluorescence intensity. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of HPTS-CTS-PFOS system rises slowly with the increasing of NaCl concentration. It was due to that the HPTS-CTS was excessive in the reaction system with the PFOS concentration of 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L. In order to reduce the background signal, this system should be performed at a low ion strength condition without adding NaCl.

Under the optimal conditions, the analytical parameters were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity increased with the concentration of PFOS. The enhanced fluorescence intensity (ΔF = F − F 0) was linear with the concentration of PFOS in range of 0.0050–2.0 μmol/L (Fig. 4b). The fitted linear data can be expressed as ΔF = 80.125c + 69.824 with linear correlation coefficient of 0.9949. LOD is defined as the concentration of the analyte at which level three times as high as the standard deviation of the noise level (3σ) is achieved. The LOD of this method was detected to be 1.0 nmol/L (0.5 μg/L).

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the method, the effects of different possible interfering substances were researched. The results are displayed in Table 1 . It illustrates that most cations and anions had negligible effect on PFOS detection expect for some metal ions such as Al3+ and Fe3+. These interfering metal ions can be removed by cation exchange resin. In addition, other surfactant, such as sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) and hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were also investigated. It was found that SDS and SDBS exert an influence to some extent. According to the previous report [28], the effect of SDS and SDBS can be removed by adding Ba2+, which has little interference in this system. As shown in Figs. S9 and S10, both SDS and SDBS have positive effect on HPTS/CTS + PFOS system. SDS and SDBS are anionic surfactants, which can bind with protonated CTS so as to release HPTS, resulting in the enhancement of fluorescence intensity. After treatment with Ba2+, the fluorescence decreases, indicating that the influence of SDS and SDBS on this system can be shielded by Ba2+. The complex particles formed by Ba2+ and SDS or SDBS can be removed through filtration with a 0.22 μm filter membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to PFOS determination in environment water
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability, the method was applied to determination of PFOS in real water samples. The river water samples were gained from the Zhujiang River in Zhongda dock. The lake water samples were obtained from Haizhu Lake. The water samples were heated to boiling, cooled down to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Afterwards, the concentration of SDS and SDBS was checked by the methylene blue spectrophotometric method [41]. Appropriate Ba2+ was added to the samples according to the concentration of SDS and SDBS. The samples were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and passed through cation exchange resin. The pretreated water samples were detected according to analytical procedures of 2.3. No PFOS was found in either river water or lake water. The recovery of the approach was studied. As listed in Table 2 , the recoveries were in range of 90.8–102% with RSD of 2.8–5.9%. The satisfactory results demonstrated that the method was applicable for water sample with good accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the method with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: Table 3 illustrates the comparison the fluorometric method based on CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy with the previous methods. Obviously, the fluorometric method based on chitosan CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy has the lowest LOD among these methods. The linear is superior to or comparable to these methods. Moreover, the method can be performed through easily physical mix, avoiding the tediousness of multi-step centrifugation or the preparation of nanoparticles. Although the LOD is still higher than regulation level for drinking water in most of countries, it is expected that if the method is coupled with sample treatment such as pre-concentration or enrichment, it will be more sensitive and holds the potential for meeting the regulation level.

In summary, PFOS has a sensitized effect on HPTS-CTS system and can enhance the fluorescence intensity of HPTS-CTS system. Therefore, a fluorometric method based on CTS-mediated “turn-on” strategy was developed for rapid detection of PFOS. The method can be performed by two steps of simple physical mix, avoiding the tedious preparation of nanoparticles. The process for detection of PFOS took less than 25 min. The linear range was 0.0050–2.0 μmol/L with LOD of 1.0 nmol/L. With advantages of rapidness, high sensitivity and good selectivity, the method provides a new choice for detection of PFOS in environmental water. It also holds the potential to assess the effects of for removal of PFOS.

Jincan He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Yuyuan Su: Validation, Software, Writing - original draft. Zijun Sun: Investigation. Runkun Zhang: Resources. Fuhai Wu: Supervision. Yan Bai: Supervision, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this study by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21804025), the Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2018A0303130079), the Science and Technology Program of Guangzhou, China (201804010329), Guangdong Provincial Engineering Research Center of Public Health Detection and Assessment (2019GCZX012), the Youth Innovation Talent Project of Education Department of Guangdong (2017KQNCX105), the Medical Science and Technology Research Foundation of Guangdong (A2018004) and the Innovation and strong School Project of Guangdong Pharmaceutical University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
